seoTitle: Post-funding: fractional senior dev 2 days/wk | Luc Rousseau
seoDescription: Before your first full-time developer: a senior two days a week as principal developer who builds, shapes the backlog, and owns technical direction, often better than a first junior hire in the same budget.

hero.title: Post-funding: before your first full-time developer
hero.lede:
You're post-funding and weighing your first technical hire. The pressure is on: ship faster, prove traction, show the team is growing. A first full-time developer feels natural; it's often the most expensive and slowest way to get solid product and technical leadership.

At two days a week, you can have a senior product engineer who is also your principal developer: prioritization, backlog, and requirements, then architecture and production code (Laravel, React, APIs). This isn't arms-length advice: I build the foundations and priority features, in a comparable budget band, with less risk if the product pivots.

blocks.title: Why the first tech hire is risky
blocks.lede: A junior or mid full-time developer means salary + payroll burden + onboarding + management, often before priorities, backlog, and requirements are clear enough. The founder still arbitrates product and tech alone while the team ships tickets without a frame. A bad hire is brutally expensive for a young company.

blocks.title: What two days a week give you

items.title: Comparable budget, senior profile
items.content: A first-hire budget can fund two days a week of a senior who has been a principal developer: priorities, backlog, and code shipped to prod, not just tickets closed.

items.title: Clear priorities, solid backlog
items.content: I prioritize features, clarify requirements and specs, and arbitrate what ships now. The team builds from a ready-to-build backlog, not vague direction.

items.title: Principal dev: I build
items.content: On those two days, I'm the principal developer: architecture, reviews, and production code. If a team already exists, I take the critical paths or guide delivery with a clear frame, with no handoff between product and build.

items.title: Flexibility if the product pivots
items.content: Before locking in an internal team, you validate pace and scope with someone who can adapt without a layoff or emergency re-hire.

items.title: Bridge to your future dev team
items.content: When you hire, you already have standards, backlog, and job criteria; I can support onboarding your first full-time developer.

blocks.title: First full-time dev vs fractional partnership
blocks.intro: Ballpark for a Quebec startup, we refine on a call.

rows.fullTimeJunior: Salary + burden + benefits; often the heaviest line in the company.
rows.fractionalSenior: Comparable band for ~40% of an experienced senior's time.

rows.fullTimeJunior: 2–4 months hiring, onboarding, learning your domain.
rows.fractionalSenior: Start in weeks; direction, backlog, and shipped code from month one.

rows.fullTimeJunior: Rarely covered by a first dev hire; founder keeps ambiguity on order and ready-to-build.
rows.fractionalSenior: Prioritization, trade-offs, backlog, and requirements before days of code are spent.

rows.fullTimeJunior: Depends on level; founder often remains default decision-maker.
rows.fractionalSenior: Explicit ownership of direction, technical trade-offs, and production quality.

rows.fullTimeJunior: Person to reposition, lay off, or replace.
rows.fractionalSenior: Scope or days adjust without a major social rupture.

rows.fullTimeJunior: 5 days of dev, often while learning your stack.
rows.fractionalSenior: 2 days where the senior is the one who builds (foundations, key features), not only who directs.

rows.fullTimeJunior: Daily presence, useful when scope is already well defined.
rows.fractionalSenior: 2 focused days + async between sessions; enough for direction, build, and unblocking when framed well.

blocks.title: Product and technical foundations
blocks.lede: Product, technical direction, and production development on your two days: clear priorities, a ready-to-build backlog, and code I ship myself (foundations, priority features), plus criteria for your first hire.
items: Laravel, REST APIs, and custom backends
React, Next.js, or Vue frontends as needed
Code standards and reviews for the future team
Documentation and handover to a first employee
Technical debt framed from day one

items.title: We need someone on code every day
items.content:
Often what you need daily is clear decisions, a backlog that moves, and code shipping, not yet five days of unsupervised dev. On two days, I structure and I build the priorities: foundations, key features, unblocking. This isn't an advisory-only role.

When volume truly justifies full-time, we'll see it together, and you'll hire with criteria already tested.

items.title: Will we still hire full-time later?
items.content: Yes, if that's the right model. My role is to get you to that moment better prepared: architecture, process, realistic job description. I can support onboarding so continuity doesn't break.

items.title: How does this work with the founder or CEO?
items.content: I partner with you: product trade-offs, backlog, reviews, ceremonies when useful. You keep the vision; I bring prioritization, requirements, and I build without turning you into a default CTO or PO.

items.title: Reachable outside the two days, or in an emergency?
items.content:
Two days a week is the rhythm for deep work, not a barrier to reaching me.

Ongoing, I make time to reply on Slack or your preferred channel. If an emergency lands outside those days, I stay reachable and step in under a shared urgency frame we define up front (scope, priority, response time), not a vague 24/7 on-call promise.

blocks.label: Discuss first tech hire
blocks.teaser: Post-raise and torn between hiring and going fractional? A 30-minute call is enough to see if two days a week with a principal developer who builds makes sense for you.
